title: The effect of home-delivered Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension (DASH) meals on the diets of older adults with cardiovascular disease
abstract: Background: Many older adults with hyperlipidemia or hypertension participate in the Older Americans Act Nutrition Program, which serves meals in community settings and delivers meals to homes. However, there is little information regarding whether therapeutic meals designed around Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension (DASH) principles have a beneficial effect on the diets of these older adults. Objective: The objective of this study was to determine the degree to which dietary change is influenced by providing 7 home-delivered therapeutic meals weekly to adults aged !60 y. Design: We conducted a 1-y randomized controlled trial in 298 persons with hyperlipidemia or hypertension, in which 50% of participants received 7 therapeutic meals per week for 12 mo. Those in need of dietary change at baseline (n = 210) were examined. Changes in intermediate DASH accordance, DASH accordance, and the nutrients that make up the DASH diet were measured by using 24-h food recalls at baseline, 6 mo, and 12 mo. Chi-square tests, t tests, and multiple regression were used to examine the association between receipt of meals and dietary change over time. Results: Participants who received meals were 20% (P = 0.001) more likely to reach intermediate DASH accordance at 6 mo and were 18% (P = 0.007) more likely to meet saturated fat accordance at 12 mo than were those who did not receive meals. When stratified by race and income, gains were marginally larger for whites and higher-income individuals. Conclusion: Delivery of 7 DASH meals per week was found to increase compliance with dietary recommendations among noncompliant older adults with cardiovascular disease.
text: Background: Many older adults with hyperlipidemia or hypertension participate in the Older Americans Act Nutrition Program, which serves meals in community settings and delivers meals to homes. However, there is little information regarding whether therapeutic meals designed around Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension (DASH) principles have a beneficial effect on the diets of these older adults. Objective: The objective of this study was to determine the degree to which dietary change is influenced by providing 7 home-delivered therapeutic meals weekly to adults aged !60 y. Design: We conducted a 1-y randomized controlled trial in 298 persons with hyperlipidemia or hypertension, in which 50% of participants received 7 therapeutic meals per week for 12 mo. Those in need of dietary change at baseline (n = 210) were examined. Changes in intermediate DASH accordance, DASH accordance, and the nutrients that make up the DASH diet were measured by using 24-h food recalls at baseline, 6 mo, and 12 mo. Chi-square tests, t tests, and multiple regression were used to examine the association between receipt of meals and dietary change over time. Results: Participants who received meals were 20% (P = 0.001) more likely to reach intermediate DASH accordance at 6 mo and were 18% (P = 0.007) more likely to meet saturated fat accordance at 12 mo than were those who did not receive meals. When stratified by race and income, gains were marginally larger for whites and higher-income individuals. Conclusion: Delivery of 7 DASH meals per week was found to increase compliance with dietary recommendations among noncompliant older adults with cardiovascular disease.
main_text: INTRODUCTION Many older adults experience hyperlipidemia and hypertension and participate in programs that provide meals in community centers or deliver them to homes. However, ,50% of all congregate meal sites and ,33% of home-delivered meal-delivery programs offer modified or therapeutic meals that vary from the regular menu (low-fat, low-cholesterol, or low-salt meals) (1) . In addition, there is little information regarding whether such meals have a beneficial effect on the dietary patterns of older adults with cardiovascular disease. The Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension (DASH) diet, developed in the mid-1990s, was prompted by the rising prev-alence of hypertension in the United States. Results from the third National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES III) indicated that 24% of US adults were hypertensive, with an increase in the prevalence and severity of hypertension with increasing age (2) . The DASH diet is characterized by an emphasis on high amounts of fruit and vegetables (high fiber), low saturated fats, low cholesterol, low sodium, and sufficient amounts of other nutrient-rich foods that have minerals known to have an effect of lowering blood pressure such as potassium, calcium, and magnesium (3, 4) . Clinical trials to evaluate the effect of the DASH diet on hypertension have shown that significant reductions in systolic and diastolic blood pressure can be achieved within as little time as 2 wk and sustained for up to 18 mo (4) (5) (6) . DASH follow-up studies have reported mixed results with regard to levels of compliance among participants in the periods after the study. In several studies, adherence to the DASH or DASH-like diet resulted in sustained reductions in blood pressure and other cardiovascular health benefits (7) (8) (9) (10) . However, other studies found little or no positive effects on cardiovascular health (4, 11, 12) . The provision of home-delivered meals to older adults with cardiovascular disease has been found to improve their overall diet quality and quality of life (13) . In the United States, the Older Americans Act Nutrition Program (OAANP) is the primary route through which nutrition services for older adults are provided. The program is administered through the US Department of Health and Human Services Administration on Aging (14) . Individuals aged !60 y are eligible to receive meals at congregate meal sites; those who receive home-delivered meals must also be homebound or otherwise isolated (15) . In an evaluation study comparing OAANP participants (recipients of congregate meals and homedelivered meals) and closely matched nonparticipants, participants were found to be better nourished (16) . However, there has also been some concern that the meals served in the OAANP may result in higher than recommended intakes of total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium (14, 17) . In this study, the effect of the provision of home-delivered therapeutically designed meals on the diets of older adults with cardiovascular disease was explored. Specifically, we examined how changes in intermediate DASH accordance, DASH accordance, and intake of protein, total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, fiber, magnesium, calcium, potassium, and sodium varied over the course of 1 y between those who received therapeutic meals and those who did not receive meals.SUBJECTS AND METHODS Data for the analysis came from a controlled, randomized, prospective design with the outcome measures taken before, in the middle of, and after the intervention. Participants remained in their assigned groups for 52 wk. Study participants were randomly assigned to 4 groups: 1) diagnosis-related diet/lifestyle literature, 2) therapeutic meals, 3) medical nutrition therapy (MNT), and 4) MNT-plus-therapeutic meals. In our analysis, we focused on differences between individuals who received therapeutic meals and individuals who did not receive meals, comparing combined groups 2 and 4 with combined groups 1 and 3. Descriptions of the 4 groups follow. (18) . Because the ADA had not finalized MNT protocols for hypertension, the RDs followed the hyperlipidemia protocol for participants with a diagnosis of either hyperlip-idemia or hypertension, with appropriate adjustments for those diagnosed with hypertension. 4) MNT-plus-therapeutic meal: The participants assigned to the MNT-plus-therapeutic meal group received both of the interventions, as described above.Eligibility criteria Individuals were eligible for the study if they were aged !60 y old and 1) received a diagnosis of hyperlipidemia and/or hypertension by a physician; 2) had appropriate storage space for meals; 3) had the mental and physical capacity to safely store, prepare, and consume meals and benefit from MNT; 4) had their physician's permission to be included in the trial; 5) were willing and able to provide blood samples, stay on the regimen, maintain a health care/medication utilization diary, and answer questions about diet and quality of life; 6) were willing to remain and fully participate in the treatment group to which they were assigned; 7) did not leave their town or city in a typical week except for acceptable reasons, as defined under the Medicare definition of homebound; 8) did not plan to be away from home for .2 wk during the intervention period; and 9) were able to communicate (speak, read, and write) in English and able to understand and willing to complete the consent process for participation. The mental and physical capacity of the subjects to safely store, prepare, and consume meals and benefit from MNT was assessed by using the Folstein Mini-Mental State Exam (MMSE). Those scoring !24 on the 30 point scale (!18 if ,8 y of schooling) were considered to be cognitively able to participate. Individuals excluded from the study had any of the following characteristics: 1) coronary artery bypass, diabetes, myocardial infarction within the 12 mo before enrollment, renal insufficiency, major surgery in the 12 wk before enrollment, unstable angina, uncompensated congestive heart failure, liver disease, pancreatic disease, current serious gastrointestinal disease (eg, colitis), or current cancer or other life-limiting diagnoses; 2) self-disclosed alcohol or drug abuse within the 24 mo before enrollment; 3) very high knowledge of dietary/lifestyle management issues required by the diagnosis, based on a knowledge questionnaire; 4) were employed full-time; or 5) had significant food allergies or were unable to eat the food offered through the project because of religious or other restrictions. A Heart Disease and Food Selection Diet/Lifestyle Knowledge Questionnaire was used to establish each participant's level of knowledge regarding their particular disease (ineligibility criterion 3). The 20 questionnaire items were initially derived from the MNT protocol for hyperlipidemia, but items were modified and refined based on recommendations from project consultants and pretesting by the study RDs with individuals mirroring the target population. Potential participants who correctly answered !18 questions were excluded, because those with a high level of nutrition knowledge would not likely benefit from MNT. We used a multifaceted recruitment approach. Sources of study participants included referrals from physicians, clinics, agencies, and churches; attendance at health fairs, flu clinics, and senior housing; and advertisements in various media sources. Because the study was designed to be a real-world clinical trial with free-living older community residents, we anticipated that participants would have several conditions and circumstances that could independently affect the outcomes. Important stratification variables included: 1) multiple cardiovascular diagnoses, compared with one; 2) use/nonuse of prescription statins; 3) score on the diet/lifestyle knowledge instrument (!7 correct items compared with ,7 items); 4) current receipt of Medicaid and/or of Medicare Parts A and B; 5) body mass index (BMI; in kg/m 2 ) of ,18.5 or .25 compared with a BMI from 18.5 to 25); 6) time since initial cardiovascular disease diagnosis (,3 y compared with !3 y); 7) completion of trial in conjunction with a primary caregiver or not; 8) age (60-74 y compared with !75 y); and 9) homebound [operationally defined as being limited to one's home or immediate area outside of the home (ie, porch, hallway, and yard) based on a simplified version of the Life-Space Assessment instrument (19) ]. Given the relatively large number of important strata and continuous recruitment-enrollment over a considerable period of time, it was not possible to know in advance the numbers of people having the stratification characteristics. Therefore, we used a "randomization with minimization" strategy (20) (21) (22) (23) . This approach began with a random assignment of the first 24 cases to the 4 groups. After the first 24 participants were randomly assigned, subsequent participants were assigned by using the minimization approach to limit differences in the stratification characteristics across groups. All aspects of the investigation were approved by the University of North Carolina at Charlotte Human Subjects Review Committee.Data collection Data on baseline participant characteristics were gathered before commencement of the intervention in each participant's home by specially trained data-collection personnel. The primary source of information for the analysis of the effect of therapeutic meals on DASH accordancy was the 24-h food recall form, which was developed by an expert nutrition consultant and the project RDs, based on key components of previously published recall forms. In addition to typical food recall information, the 24-h food recall form included items on the preparation methods of foods consumed, vitamin and mineral supplements, and meal consumption time. In addition, a separate 12-item food-frequency section was included at the end of the third pass for each recall to allow interviewers to verify food intake and prompt individuals for items that are eaten frequently but are not reported by the third pass. The recall form was tested for content validity by a group of health care professionals and was subsequently modified to further optimize its utility. These forms were administered by using a multiple pass method by trained project staff at the beginning of each participant's intervention, at midterm and at the conclusion of the intervention (24) . One recall was administered at each collection point. Information from the recalls was analyzed by an RD with experience in using Nutritionist Pro to determine nutritional intake. Intermediate DASH scores and DASH scores based on the methods presented by Mellen et al (11) were calculated (Table 1 ). The DASH diet consists of dietary recommendations for 9 nutrients (protein, total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, fiber, magnesium, calcium, potassium, and sodium). Continuous variables for protein, total fat, and saturated fat as a percentage of total energy were developed. For cholesterol, fiber, magnesium, calcium, potassium, and sodium, intake per 1000 calories was computed. In addition, binary variables indicating whether the participant met the target intake were constructed for each nutrient, based on the intermediate DASH and DASH classifications, as discussed below. An overall DASH score was calculated, ie, meeting a nutrient target added 1 point to the score for a total possible individual score of 9. DASH accordance was defined as having a DASH score of !4.5. An intermediate DASH score was also calculated, ie, meeting the DASH target for a nutrient added 1 point to the total score and meeting the intermediate DASH target added 0.5 points to the total score. Intermediate DASH accordance was defined as having an intermediate DASH score of !4.5 (Table 1 ) (11) . In an effort to examine potential group differences in responses to therapeutic meals, we considered the measures discussed above for the full sample and for 2 subgroups. First, we examined those with incomes .165% or 165% of the federal poverty level. Access to therapeutic meals may have a larger effect on low-income individuals, for which food is a larger part of the household budget (25) . However, low-income individuals may also face higher barriers to dietary change (26) , including financial limitations that restrict the translation of learning about healthy foods through the therapeutic meal to other components of the daily diet. Second, separately we examined those who self-identified as white and those who identified themselves as nonwhite. Race may be correlated with socioeconomic status and social norms around food preparation and diet, which may help or hinder attempts at dietary change (27) , independent of income. Of the 321 individuals originally enrolled in the study, 298 provided data at all 3 measurement points. Of the 298 with complete data, the study sample included only those who were not intermediate DASH accordant at baseline (n = 210). Excluding those who were intermediate DASH accordant at baseline allowed us to focus on those who were in need of dietary change at baseline, as measured by DASH diet guidelines. As a result, the analysis focused on the importance of the provision of therapeutic meals for older persons in need of dietary change.Statistical analysis To consider the effect of therapeutic meals on compliance with the DASH diet, we began with an examination of the We also considered DASH accordancy in the key nutrient categories at these 3 time points. For instance, we determined the percentage of individuals who had diets that were low enough in saturated fat at 6 mo to qualify as meeting saturated fat intake recommendations. In addition to considering the percentage of individuals who were DASH accordant (in full or in part) at different points in time, we also examined the total DASH score and continuous versions of the nutrient elements that make up the score, such as the percentage of energy from saturated fat. It is important to note that all analyses were based on intention to treat, in which some individuals who declined to continue to participate in the study continued to allow for data collection and were included in the analysis. For the full sample and subgroups that did and did not get the therapeutic meals, we used chi-square tests (for the percentages) and t tests (for the continuous measures) to determine whether unadjusted changes from baseline to 6 mo and changes from 6 to 12 mo were statistically significant. Difference-in-differences (DD) models were used to estimate the effects of the therapeutic meal intervention. The basic DD model compares the difference in outcomes over time for a control group to the difference in outcomes during the same period for an experimental group subject to a particular intervention during the study period. The advantage of this design is that the change for the control group picks up any naturally occurring changes, whereas the experimental group change reflects both the (same) naturally occurring change and the effect of the intervention, thereby allowing for an accurate measurement of the effect of the intervention. In our study, a DD model was used to predict the effect of the provision of therapeutic meals by examining changes over time in continuous outcomes (such as DASH score or percentage of energy from saturated fat) for the groups that did and did not get the meals. The basic model is represented by the following equation: Y it ¼ b 0 þ b 1 T M i þ b 2 6Month t þ b 3 12Month t þb 4 ðT M i Þð6Month t Þ þ b 5 ðT M i Þð12Month t Þ þb 6 MNT i þ b 7 ðMNT i Þð6Month t Þ þb 8 ðMNT i Þð12Month t Þ þ e it ð1Þ where i is the individual, t is the period of measurement (baseline, 6 mo, or 12 mo), Y it is the outcome (such as DASH score), TM is a binary indicator that is equal to 1 for individuals who received therapeutic meals and 0 otherwise, 6Month is a binary indicator for the measurement occurring at 6 mo, and 12Month is a binary indicator for the measurement occurring at 12 mo. We also controlled for whether the individual received MNT with MNT , which is a binary indicator that is equal to 1 for individuals who received MNT and equal to 0 otherwise, and for interactions between MNT and the time indicators. The 2 interaction terms on the therapeutic meals variables, (TM) (6Month) and (TM)(12Month), are of central focus, because they provide an accurate measurement of the effect of the intervention at 6 and 12 mo, respectively. If the coefficients on these variables are positive and statistically significant, it suggests that the provision of therapeutic meals leads to an increase in the outcome variable, such as DASH score, after receiving the meals for both 6 and 12 mo. For the measures that were binary in nature, such as whether an individual's diet was DASH accordant or whether the individual met the DASH standard for saturated fat intake, a logit version of the DD model was used to examine how the therapeutic meals may have changed the probability that the individual was DASH accordant or the probability that the individual met the standard for a nutrient such as saturated fat. Unlike in the linear model, in the logit model the coefficient on the interaction variable is not directly interpretable (28) . Therefore, to compute the average effect of the treatment (the receipt of therapeutic meals), we considered the change in the predicted probability that occurs when we imputed the 2 values for the TM binary variable to the entire sample and computed the mean difference in the predicted probability of each outcome for those who did and did not get the meals. For inference, we bootstrapped the SEs for the changes in the predicted probabilities considered. As a result, the estimates presented below for the logit model are analogous to those presented in the linear regression case; for example, we presented the change in the probability (in terms of percentageRESULTS Of the 321 individuals enrolled in the study, 22 withdrew and did not permit complete data collection. Of the 321 individuals, 210 participants (full sample) were not intermediate DASH accordant at baseline and provided data at all 3 measurement points. Of these, the majority were white (61.0%), were women (82.9%), had an income .165% of the poverty level (56.6%), and received home-delivered therapeutic meals (50.5%) (Table 2 ). More than 50% of the participants had both hypertension and hyperlipidemia diagnosed by a physician (52.0%), nearly 30% had only hypertension (28.2%), and the remaining '20% had only hyperlipidemia (19.8%). Of the 210 participants who were not DASH accordant at baseline, 21 participants (10%) were in the intention-to-treat category, because they allowed us to continue to collect data after withdrawing from the study at some point during the year. Of the full sample, most were meeting nutrient targets in only 2 categories at baseline: saturated fat and calcium (Table 3 ). There was a change in the proportion of individuals who were intermediate DASH accordant and DASH accordant from baseline to the 6-mo follow-up. In the full sample, there was a significant increase in the percentage of participants meeting individual nutrient targets from baseline to 6 mo for most of the nutrients: protein, total fat, magnesium, potassium, and sodium. However, the percentage of participants meeting the protein and total fat targets were attenuated by 12 mo. When stratified by receipt of meals, there was a significant increase in the percentage intermediate DASH accordant and DASH accordant from baseline to 6 mo among those who received therapeutic meals (Table 3 ). At 12 mo, the percentage attenuated but this change was not significantly different from the percentage at 6 mo. Also, among those who received therapeutic meals, there was a significant increase in the percentage of participants who met nutrient targets for protein, saturated fat, total fat, magnesium, and potassium. For individuals who did not get meals, there were much smaller gains from baseline to 6 mo in the proportion of individuals that were intermediate DASH accordant, DASH accordant, or met the nutrient targets. Whereas there generally appeared to be no significant change in the percentage of those meeting nutrient targets between the 6-and 12-mo measurement points, there was one exception: a significant decrease in the percentage of meal recipients meeting targets for protein from 6 to 12 mo. In general, the patterns for the mean values for the continuous measures of DASH accordancy and nutrient intake (Table 4 ) mirrored those of the proportions (Table 3 ). The mean (6SD) intermediate DASH score (at baseline) was 2.5 6 1.1, and the DASH score (at baseline) was 1.4 6 0.9 (Table 4 ). Of the full sample, there was a significant increase in intake of protein and a decrease in intake of total fat and saturated fat from baseline to 6 mo. There was also an increase in cholesterol, fiber, magnesium, calcium, and potassium intakes from baseline to 6 mo. There was no significant change in the intermediate DASH score, DASH score, or nutrient intakes from 6 to 12 mo. Among participants who received the therapeutic meals, there was a significant increase in intermediate DASH score, DASH score, and protein, fiber, magnesium, calcium, and potassium intakes from baseline to 6 mo (Table 4 ). Participants who did not receive therapeutic meals had an increase in the average intermediate DASH score and DASH score; a significant increase in protein, magnesium, and potassium intakes; and a decrease in total fat from baseline to 6 mo. Again, the changes tended to be smaller in magnitude for the group that did not get the meals. There was no change in intermediate DASH score, DASH score, or nutrient intake from 6 to 12 mo among those who received meals and those who did not. For both sets of multivariate DD estimates, for the sake of brevity, we report results for all binary or continuous variables for which there was at least one significant result for the full sample or one of the subgroups. In the DD analysis of the binary variables that indicated that a DASH intake target was or was not met, participants who received meals had a 20 percentage point higher probability of being intermediate DASH accordant at 6 mo (P = 0.001) than did those who did not get therapeutic meals (Table 5 ). At 6 mo, those receiving therapeutic meals had a 10 percentage point higher probability of meeting the saturated fat intake target of the DASH diet. In addition, those who received therapeutic meals were more likely to meet saturated fat and potassium goals at 12 mo than were participants who did not receive meals. When stratified by race and income, receipt of meals increased the probability of being intermediate DASH accordant at 6 mo among all groups, but the effects were modestly smaller for nonwhites and lower-income individuals. When stratified by race, differences in the effect of therapeutic meals on meeting nutrient targets emerged. Receipt of therapeutic meals among nonwhites increased the probability of DASH accordance at 6 mo and the probability of meeting the fiber target at 6 mo. In contrast, among whites, receipt of therapeutic meals was associated with an increased probability of meeting both protein and total fat targets at 6 mo and of meeting the potassium and saturated fat targets at 12 mo. When stratified by income, we also found differences in the effect of the therapeutic meals on the probability of meeting the nutrient targets. Receipt of meals increased the probability of meeting the protein and potassium targets at 6 mo among lower-income participants. However, among higher-income participants, receipt of therapeutic meals resulted in a higher probability of intermediate DASH accordance at 12 mo, of meeting the fiber target at 6 mo, and of meeting the saturated fat target at 12 mo. Multiple regression was used in the context of a DD framework to examine the influence of receipt of therapeutic meals on continuous measures of DASH score and nutrient intake (Table 6 ). Receipt of meals was not significantly associated with DASH score or nutrient intake among the full sample. Among nonwhites, however, those who received therapeutic meals had higher intermediate DASH scores, lower cholesterol intakes, and higher fiber intakes at 6 mo than did nonwhites who did not receive meals. Receipt of meals was not significantly associated with DASH score or nutrient intake among whites. After stratification by income, receipt of meals was not statistically significant for DASH score or nutrient intake for low-income individuals. However, the receipt of therapeutic meals results in significant decreases in saturated fat intake at 12 mo in higherincome individuals.DISCUSSION In the current study, we examined the effect of providing 7 therapeutically designed, home-delivered meals per week on the diets of older adults with cardiovascular disease who were not consuming an intermediate DASH-accordant diet. Using 3 data measurement points and a randomized controlled study design, we found that therapeutic meals improved the probability that the older adults in the study had diets that were intermediate DASH accordant. This result was found for the full sample and for all groups when stratified by race and income, although the gains in the probability of being DASH accordant were marginally larger for whites and higher-income individuals. Whereas some significant gains in the probability of meeting nutrient targets were seen at 12 mo, most of the gains occurred between the baseline and 6-mo measurement point. When examining the continuous measures of DASH scores and nutrient intake, nonwhites saw significant improvements from baseline to 6 mo with reductions in cholesterol intake occurring at the 12-mo mark. The continuous measures better capture the magnitude of dietary change, indicating that the largest changes in nutrient intake occurred among nonwhites who received therapeutic meals.  Values were derived from a multiple logistic regression and controlled for receipt of medical nutrition therapy and time trends by using a difference-indifferences approach. 2 Values represent the estimated change in the probability of meeting the nutrient or accordancy target attributable to the receipt of therapeutic meals. For example, for the full sample at 6 mo, the group who received therapeutic meals had a .20 percentage point higher probability of being intermediate DASH accordant than did the group who did not receive meals. 3 Reflects changes associated with receipt of meals; P 0.05 indicates significance. In the unadjusted analysis of the full sample, study participants were more likely to be DASH accordant (overall or at the nutrient level), regardless of whether or not they received the therapeutic meal intervention. This finding reinforces the need for the DD approach used in this study, which controls for factors that affect both the intervention and control groups equally. There are 2 factors that may be driving gains by both the treatment and control groups in DASH accordancy. First, the results may be influenced by the participants' desire to receive approval by the researchers involved. This social approval bias phenomenon has been documented in the self-reporting of fruit and vegetable consumption, for instance (29) . Second, as mentioned above in the exclusion criteria, all of the participants in the study were screened for participation by using the Heart Disease and Food Selection Diet/Lifestyle Knowledge Questionnaire. This questionnaire was administered by a registered dietitian, and, after completing the 20-item questionnaire on food and lifestyle choices, all participants were permitted an opportunity to ask questions about items missed. During this process, all participants may have gained some knowledge that led to behavior change, regardless of study arm. A strength of the analysis was the inclusion of intention-totreat cases in the study sample. The study had little attrition, and most individuals who agreed to participate in the year-long study continued to permit data collection, even if they declined the therapeutic meals offered by the study. By including intention-totreat cases, any results of the meals are attenuated. Therefore, statistically significant findings associated with the meals may be viewed as particularly robust. Whereas our results shed light on the relation between providing one therapeutic meal per day and overall dietary change for older adults with cardiovascular disease, there are several areas that warrant further investigation. First, although the meals resulted in some increased compliance with specific health requirements for this group, more compliancewith dietary requirements might occur if the meals were personalized to individual tastes. Similarly, in a nontrial setting, many older adults with cardiovascular disease also have diabetes (30) , which may require further modification of the meals (31) . Modification for multiple comorbidities may result in better health outcomes for individuals receiving the meals. In addition, this intervention provided only one meal per day. Greater gains in terms of dietary change may be seen if a greater proportion of daily intake was covered by the meals, and future researchers may wish to consider this. Finally, although we showed that some dietary changes attributable to the therapeutic meals continued to persist at 12 mo, the most robust changes appeared to occur between baseline and 6 mo. Future research might consider this phenomenon explicitly, by interviewing individuals regarding reasons for reversion to previous behaviors. In addition, research into mechanisms that may prolong dietary change, such as increased variety of meals, more involvement in the meal design process, or increased educational opportunities related to dietary change, may prove enlightening. In sum, the literature indicates that short-term and long-term adherence to the DASH diet can have a positive effect on health outcomes. In this study, one-third of the recommended daily nutrient intake was provided to participating older adults with cardiovascular disease for 1 y. These therapeutic meals had 1 Values were derived from a multiple logistic regression and controlled for receipt of medical nutrition therapy and time trends by using a difference-indifferences approach. 2 Values represent the coefficients on the interaction terms at 6 and 12 mo for the interactions between the period indicator and the receipt of therapeutic meals. 3 Reflects changes associated with receipt of meals; P 0.05 indicates significance. a significant effect on DASH diet accordancy at 6 and 12 mo, with the largest gains in nutrient intake changes observed for nonwhites at the 6-mo mark. These findings suggest that all OAANPs should provide therapeutic meals to the participants, because these meals may have important spillover effects on other aspects of the participants' diets and, ultimately, on health outcomes for the many older adults with cardiovascular disease.TABLE 1 1 Daily nutrient targets for Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension (DASH) scores1 Intermediate DASH title: In type 2 diabetes, randomisation to advice to follow a low-carbohydrate diet transiently improves glycaemic control compared with advice to follow a low-fat diet producing a similar weight loss
abstract: Aims/hypothesis The study aimed to compare the effects of a 2 year intervention with a low-fat diet (LFD) or a lowcarbohydrate diet (LCD), based on four group meetings to achieve compliance. Methods This was a prospective randomised parallel trial involving 61 adults with type 2 diabetes consecutively recruited in primary care and randomised by drawing ballots. Patients that did not speak Swedish could not be recruited. The primary outcomes in this non-blinded study were weight and HbA 1c . Patients on the LFD aimed for 55-60 energy per cent (E%) and those on LCD for 20 E% from carbohydrate. Results The mean BMI and HbA 1c of the participants were 32.7±5.4 kg/m 2 and 57.0±9.2 mmol/mol, respectively. No patients were lost to follow-up. Weight loss did not differ between groups and was maximal at 6 months: LFD -3.99± 4.1 kg (n031); LCD -4.31±3.6 kg (n030); p<0.001 within groups. At 24 months, patients on the LFD had lost -2.97± 4.9 kg and those on LCD -2.34±5.1 kg compared with baseline (p00.002 and p00.020 within groups, respectively). HbA 1c fell in the LCD group only (LCD at 6 months -4.8± 8.3 mmol/mol, p00.004, at 12 months -2.2±7.7 mmol/mol, p00.12; LFD at 6 months -0.9±8.8 mmol/mol, p00.56). At 6 months, HDL-cholesterol had increased with the LCD (from 1.13±0.33 mmol/l to 1.25±0.47 mmol/l, p00.018) while LDL-cholesterol did not differ between groups. Insulin doses were reduced in the LCD group (0 months, LCD 42±65 E, LFD 39±51 E; 6 months, LCD 30±47 E, LFD 38±48 E; p00.046 for between-group change). Conclusions/interpretation Weight changes did not differ between the diet groups, while insulin doses were reduced significantly more with the LCD at 6 months, when compliance was good. Thus, aiming for 20% of energy intake from carbohydrates is safe with respect to cardiovascular risk compared with the traditional LFD and this approach could constitute a treatment alternative.
text: Aims/hypothesis The study aimed to compare the effects of a 2 year intervention with a low-fat diet (LFD) or a lowcarbohydrate diet (LCD), based on four group meetings to achieve compliance. main_text: Introduction The prevalence of type 2 diabetes is increasing worldwide, and this is likely to be a consequence of the increasing prevalence of obesity. Weight loss in obesity generally leads to improvement in cardiovascular risk factors and glycaemic control [1, 2] . However, few randomised studies have specifically targeted type 2 diabetes to compare the effect of different diets in this respect. Traditionally, a low-fat diet (LFD) has been recommended [3] to patients with type 2 diabetes as a means to lose weight and, in particular, a low intake of saturated fat has been advocated [4, 5] . Interestingly, in a randomised 2 year study from Israel that achieved good compliance, a high-fat diet was shown to induce better weight reduction and improved blood lipid levels than a traditional LFD in obese participants, while the subgroup of patients with diabetes who were randomised to the highfat diet exhibited the largest reduction in HbA 1c levels [6] . From a physiological point of view it could be argued that carbohydrate should be avoided to achieve good glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes, as a typical feature of type 2 diabetes is the combination of reduced insulin sensitivity and the failure of beta cells to provide adequate amounts of insulin to handle glucose derived from carbohydrate in the diet. However, when the macronutrient composition is changed in a diet by reducing carbohydrate, the energy from this source is primarily replaced by that from fat, as a high energy intake from protein is hard to achieve in the long term. Thus, a low-carbohydrate diet (LCD) is quite similar to one with a high intake of fat, which has traditionally been linked with increased risk for arteriosclerosis, particularly when large amounts of saturated fat are consumed. However, recent data have challenged the concept of the risks with a high-fat diet. In a Swedish observational study in 28,000 middle-aged individuals, neither a high fat intake nor an intake of large amounts of saturated fat (22 E%) was linked with an increased risk for cardiovascular disease [7, 8] . Most earlier studies examining high-fat diets in patients with type 2 diabetes have had limitations, such as a high dropout frequency [9] [10] [11] [12] , lack of randomisation [13] [14] [15] , or a maximum duration of 12 months [11, 12, 16] . Another problem for the clinical feasibility of studies involving patients with type 2 diabetes has been the scale of the resources necessary to achieve compliance with the tested diets; typically, numerous individual meetings with trained dietitians are held during the studies [11, 16, 17] . If these measures were to become part of routine healthcare for such a common disease, there would be considerable economic implications for the medical provider. We performed a randomised study in patients with type 2 diabetes to compare glycaemic control, weight loss and cardiovascular risk factors based on the advice of a lowcarbohydrate or a traditional LFD. In contrast to most previous studies, the patients randomised to the LCD were not asked to avoid saturated fat. The interventions were based on four group meetings with a duration of 60 min each for the first year; no further group meetings during the remaining 12 months were held. Our hypothesis was that the high-fat diet would improve glycaemic control more efficiently than the traditional LFD in a study in which few resources were allocated for achieving compliance, so that clinical use of the protocol would be realistic for many providers of care.Methods The study was conducted in two primary healthcare centres in the cities of Motala and Borensberg, located in southeast Sweden. Patients who fulfilled the criteria for participation were contacted individually by one of three study nurses. The nurses had also been responsible for the care of these potential participants ahead of the study start. The inclusion criteria were a diagnosis of type 2 diabetes treated with diet with or without additional oral glucose-lowering medication, incretin-based therapy or insulin. There were no weight or age exclusion criteria, but patients who had difficulties understanding the Swedish language, were suffering from severe mental disease or malignant disease, or who were abusing drugs could not participate in the study. The patients were randomised to either an LCD or a traditional LFD, both with an energy content of 6,694 kJ/day (1,600 kcal/day) for women or 7,531 kJ/day (1,800 kcal/day) for men. Randomisation was not stratified and was based on drawing blinded ballots. The LCD had an energy content where 50 energy per cent (E%) was from fat, 20 E% was from carbohydrate and 30 E% was from protein. The LFD had a nutrient composition that was similar to that traditionally recommended for the treatment of type 2 diabetes in Sweden, with 30 E% from fat (less than 10 E% from saturated fat), 55-60 E% from carbohydrate and 10-15 E% from protein. Group information was used to inform the randomised patients about which food items to choose from, and this was given at baseline, and 2, 6 and 12 months by two different physicians. One dedicated dietitian provided the participants from both groups with suitable recipes at each group meeting, and was also available consecutively during the trial for questions from the participants. However, all the information necessary was provided at the group information meetings, and thus no individual meetings with the dietitian were scheduled as part of the general protocol. Menus for 1 week were provided to the participants as meal suggestions by the dietitian. Each patient had the same dedicated nurse during the whole study period and the nurses could also provide information about food to the patients during regular consultations. The patients were recommended to check plasma glucose levels before and after meals after initiation of the study to allow for proper adjustment of medication to avoid hypoglycaemia. No information was given to change the level of physical activity. As the duration of the trial was 2 years, and because individuals with high risk for cardiovascular events were recruited, it was judged to be unethical not to be allowed to adjust medication to avoid cardiovascular disease in the study. The physician responsible for each patient at the primary healthcare centre was thus allowed to adjust hypolipidaemic and antihypertensive medications consecutively in the trial. Investigations of anthropometrics and laboratory tests were performed at baseline and at 6, 12 and 24 months, and patients were also asked to fill out questionnaires of wellbeing at these time points. Diet records were also performed at these four visits, with one additional recording at 3 months. The diet records were conducted during 3 consecutive days, of which 1 day was a Saturday or a Sunday. The participants were provided with dedicated scales and notebooks from the organisers with which to weigh and record all food items that were consumed during these periods (food frequency questionnaires were not used). Sagittal abdominal diameter was measured with a sliding beam set square as the highest abdominal level above the upper surface of the corresponding bed. The laboratory tests were analysed at the Department of Clinical Chemistry at the University Hospital of Linköping as part of clinical routine analyses, and fasting LDL-cholesterol was thus calculated using the Friedewald formula. Statistics Statistical calculations were made using PASW 18.0 software (SPSS, Chicago, IL, USA). Linear correlations were calculated as stated in the text. Comparisons within and between groups were made with Student's paired and unpaired two-tailed t tests or as stated in the results section. The mean (SD) is given unless otherwise stated. Statistical significance was considered to be present at the 5% level (p≤0.05). ANOVA with repeated measures was used for calculations of the changes during the total duration of the study. The size of the study was based on an earlier 6 month pilot study of 28 participants with type 2 diabetes who were randomised to the same diets as in the study presented in this paper. Twenty individuals completed the pilot study and both diet groups achieved similar weight reductions, while HbA 1c levels tended to be lowered in the low-carbohydrate group only, without taking change in medication into account (low carbohydrate, p00.068; low-fat group, p00.8). Based on these results, the study sample was increased to at least 30 individuals in each group in the present study. None of the participants in the pilot study participated in the trial presented in this paper. The study duration of 24 months was requested by the Regional Ethics Committee of Linköping.Ethical approval The study was approved by the Regional Ethics Committee of Linköping and performed in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Written informed consent was obtained from all participating individuals. The study was registered with trial number NCT01005498 at ClinicalTrials.gov.Results Total study population The study nurses invited 72 consecutive patients to participate in the study from the autumn of 2008 to the start of the clinical trial in March 2009. Ten patients declined because they judged the study to be too timeconsuming and one patient believed that a high-fat diet might be hazardous. The remaining 61 patients entered the study, but three in the LFD group and four in the LCD group expressed that they had severe difficulty following the prescribed diets and were not willing to participate in the group meetings. Data on the main outcomes from these seven patients were used in statistical analyses according to intention-to-treat analysis (Table 1 ; see also Fig. 1 for flow diagram). In the low-fat group, the mean age was 62.7±11 years, there were 13 men and 18 women and the mean duration of known diabetes was 8.8±6.2 years. Corresponding figures for the low-carbohydrate group were 61.2±9.5 years, 14 men and 16 women and a duration of known diabetes of 9.8±5.5 years. Age, sex distribution and known duration of diabetes did not differ between the groups (all p>0.5). At baseline, two patients in the low-fat group and two in the low-carbohydrate group were treated with diet only, 13 in the low-fat group and 15 in the lowcarbohydrate group were using oral glucose-lowering medication only, and 11 in the low-fat group and 10 in the lowcarbohydrate group were treated with a combination of insulin and oral medication. At 24 months, 14 individuals (four in the LFD group and ten in the LCD group) did not provide diet records, but data on the main outcomes were used in calculations. The dietitian had individual consultations with four patients in the low-carbohydrate group and three patients in the low-fat group; information was otherwise only provided during the group meetings. These individual contacts all took part during the first 12 months of the study. Table 1 shows anthropometrics, laboratory variables and medication from baseline throughout the study. The recorded intake of energy from fat and carbohydrate differed between the groups (Table 2 ). There was no difference in weight reduction between the groups at 6 months (LFD group -4.0±4.1 kg; LCD group -4.3± 3.6 kg; p00.75 for difference in change between groups and p < 0.001 within either group; Table 1 and Fig. 2 ). There were also no statistically significant differences in weight reduction between groups after adjustment for baseline carbohydrate or fat intake (all p>0.5).  As there were no changes in simvastatin dose, the t test is not applicable A significant reduction in HbA 1c was seen at 6 months in the low-carbohydrate group only (Table 1 ). However, HbA 1c levels gradually returned to baseline levels after 6 months as shown in Fig. 3 and Table 1 . The change in HbA 1c level at 6 months compared with baseline was not statistically significant between the groups (p00.089). Reductions in oral medication and insulin dose were made consecutively to avoid hypoglycaemia, and the reduction in insulin was statistically significant only in the low-carbohydrate group at 6 months (Table 1 ). This change in the average insulin dose was statistically significant between the two groups at 6 months (p00.046). There were no significant differences between groups regarding office blood pressure levels in the study (Table 1 ). At 6 months, four patients in the LCD group and one patient in the LFD group had started on treatment with statins or had their former dose increased (see also Table 1 ). Corresponding figures for these medical adjustments during the entire study period were nine patients in the LCD group and seven patients receiving the LFD. By the end of the trial, 54 of 61 patients received lipid-lowering medication. At 6 months, the low-carbohydrate group showed significantly increased levels of HDL-cholesterol (Table 1 ; p00.018 for change within group, p 00.077 for change between groups). No patient suffered cardiovascular disease or other serious adverse events during the study. The results of the dietary records are shown in Table 2 . During the first 6 months, adherence to the proposed diet was comparatively good in both groups as judged by mean values of macronutrient intake (Table 2 ). The low-fat group did not significantly change the macronutrient composition during the study, while there was an increase in energy from fat in those randomised to LCD (Table 2 ). Despite reduction of energy intake, total intake of fat showed no reduction in the low-carbohydrate group, which was in contrast to the low-fat group (Table 2 ). The percentage of energy intake from saturated fat increased in the low-carbohydrate group throughout the whole study, but there were no differences between the groups regarding energy intake, according to the diet records (Table 2 ). Completers analysis Additional analyses were made according to compliance with energy intake. Table 3 shows results for only those patients who consumed ≤6,694 kJ/day (1,600 kcal/day) for women or ≤7,531 kJ/day (1,800 kcal/ day) for men according to the last diet record at 24 months; it includes 17 patients in the low-fat group and 18 patients in the low-carbohydrate group. At 24 months the patients randomised to the LFD weighed 3.1±4.3 kg less than at baseline and the corresponding figure for the lowcarbohydrate group was 3.5±4.0 kg. This suggests that, in particular, the high-fat group had lower compliance at this time point, which affected weight gain in the total LCD-cohort (i.e. -3.5±4.0 kg in completers compared with -2.3±5.1 kg in the total cohort). While sagittal abdominal diameter was stable in the low-fat group, this was reduced by 2 cm in the lowcarbohydrate group. However, the low-fat group with energy compliance had reduced systolic and diastolic blood pressure, effects lacking in those randomised to the high-fat diet (Table 3 ). A second completers analysis based on compliance with fat intake is available in the electronic supplementary material (ESM; compliance defined as: ≤35 E% from fat in the low-fat group, n020, or ≥45 E% from fat for the lowcarbohydrate group, n012, χ 2 between groups for compliance with intake of fat p00.06). Similar results to those in the completers analysis according to energy intake were found d Denotes statistically significant difference between changes in the two groups e Denotes statistically significant difference between groups at baseline in this post-hoc calculation for sagittal abdominal diameter and blood pressure. HDL-cholesterol increased from 1.11± 0.36 to 1.46±0.59 mmol/l (p00.001 for all time points) in those compliant with the LCD according to fat intake, while corresponding figures for the LFD were 1.05±0.26 mmol/l at baseline to 1.17±0.25 mmol/l at 24 months (p00.006 within group and p00.016 for change between groups).Discussion Our study did not confirm the finding that weight reduction is more efficient in individuals following an LCD than in those following an LFD, as has been found in some previous trials [6, 9, 12, [18] [19] [20] . An important difference in our study compared with these earlier studies [6, 9, 12, [18] [19] [20] was that we used comparatively fewer resources to achieve compliance. In our study, only four group meetings were offered during the first 12 months of the trial. The rationale for this design was to make the results more applicable to regular clinic care in which educational activities such as group meetings can be offered as a means to improve glycaemic control. No patients were lost to follow-up and data on glycaemic control were complete at 24 months, while data on weight were lacking for only one participant at this time point. This outcome of our study left minimal room for the selection of participants, who did indeed find either of the diets suitable, to affect the main outcomes. Our findings indicate that if patients are randomised to an LCD compared with an LFD with resources used to achieve changes in diet composition that are readily available for many providers of care, both diets induce similar weight reductions. This was also in line with the finding that both groups reported similar energy intake during the study. Westman et al have reported more efficient weight reduction with an LCD after 6 months when compared with an LFD [9] . In that study, diet information was facilitated, compared with our design, by lack of energy restriction in the low-carbohydrate group. This could have affected the more beneficial findings compared with our study regarding weight loss. Also, Westman et al had a total of 18 group meetings during their 6 month study, and this could have affected the outcome. Information on increased exercise was also part of their lifestyle change programme, but was not included in ours. Specifically, we aimed to study the effects of macronutrient composition on glycaemic control and cardiovascular risk factors, which was why we aimed to achieve no differences in energy intake in the information we gave to the participants. Interestingly, we did find an increase in HDL-cholesterol after 6 months and a specific reduction in HbA 1c levels in the low-carbohydrate group only, which suggests that these effects are dependent on macronutrient composition per se; this is in line with the findings of Westman et al [9] . We also acknowledge that we might have achieved better weight reduction if a design similar to that used in 'Weight Watchers' programmes had been incorporated. For regular care provided by the Swedish tax-based system, incentives used in Weight Watchers, such as public display of the body weight results of the participants, would not be feasible for general use in clinic care because of patient privacy. Also, one should keep in mind that there is selection and incentive in such commercially run programmes, as participants are willing to pay to participate. However, effective weight loss in a Weight Watchers group was recently shown in a study even when the cost for participation was reimbursed by the study organisers [21] . Fig. 2 Comparison of weight reduction with an LCD, aiming to achieve 20 E% from carbohydrate (squares), and an LFD (circles), aiming for 55-60 E% from carbohydrates, during 2 years in patients with type 2 diabetes. The weight reduction did not differ between the groups (p00.33 for all time points) Fig. 3 Comparison of the reduction in HbA 1c levels following an LCD with the aim of achieving 20 E% from carbohydrate (squares), and an LFD (circles), aiming for 55-60 E% from carbohydrate, over 2 years in patients with type 2 diabetes. The reduction in HbA 1c level was statistically significant within the low-carbohydrate group (p00.005 for all time points), but did not differ between the groups when compared at all time points (p00.76). To convert values for HbA 1c in mmol/mol into %, divide by 10.929 and add 2. 15 Although patients in our study who had been randomised to the low-carbohydrate group reported a lower intake of carbohydrates at baseline compared with the low-fat group, this was unrelated to weight changes in statistical analyses. In retrospect, this group difference in reported intake of macronutrients between the groups might have been a consequence of the participants being informed of the randomisation results before the diet record at baseline was performed. Consequently, some participants may have adjusted their diet to make it similar to that to which they had been allocated, ahead of the first group information meeting. Unfortunately the baseline difference was not elucidated until the end of the trial and it was thus judged to be of little meaning to ask participants with little intake of energy from carbohydrates in the low-carbohydrate group at baseline whether this was a consequence of the randomisation, which had occurred more than 2 years earlier. The largest changes in macronutrient intake were seen in patients randomised to the low-carbohydrate group. Indeed, patients in the low-fat group had the same macronutrient composition at baseline as during the study, suggesting that this was indeed a traditional diet and that they, according to the diet records, had been given similar diet recommendations earlier. The patients following the LCD increased the percentage of energy intake from both total and saturated fat throughout the 24 months of the trial according to diet records, in line with the study protocol. At 6 months, when weight reduction was most pronounced, only the LCD group had changes in blood lipid levels in the form of increased HDL-cholesterol. However, during the study there had also been changes in lipid-lowering therapy that make these findings inconclusive regarding whether they solely depended on changes in diet. At the end of the trial, several patients had been newly started on lipid-lowering therapy. This is an obvious limitation of our trial from a mechanistic point of view, but it was a consequence of our efforts to limit the resources necessary for the diets to be implemented in regular primary care, to allow the methods to translate easily to real-life application. However, as 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase inhibitors (statins) mainly affect LDL-cholesterol levels and, as earlier trials have also found that diets high in fat elevate HDL-cholesterol to a greater extent than high-carbohydrate diets in type 2 diabetes [9, 11, 16] , we find it likely that the increase in HDL-cholesterol in our trial was mainly an effect of the change in diet. We acknowledge that the general applicability of our study results might be limited because of the high participation rate that was achieved. The study nurses had also taken care of the same patients ahead of the study start and when identifying potential participants according to inclusion and exclusion criteria it cannot be excluded that, prior to the study, they might have discharged patients judged not to have been suitable participants for various reasons. Another potential explanation for the high participation rate was that the study protocol was not very time-consuming for the patients as it involved only four group meetings. We also acknowledge the problems with diet records. Although diet records with notebooks and scales can be more detailed and precise than standardised food frequency questionnaires, results from surveys of food intake have low reproducibility and, in particular, there are systematic errors in underreporting energy intake [22] . Thus, total energy intake might not be accurate in our study, but the lowering of HbA 1c in only the low-carbohydrate group at 6 months and also differences in HDL-cholesterol changes at similar weight reductions suggest that the groups did indeed change their macronutrient intake differently in our trial. The analyses of outcome in the participants who were compliant with either energy intake or with the E% from fat implied better long-term effects on weight loss than in the total cohort analysed on an intention-to-treat basis. Although this was a post-hoc analysis, and thus data should be interpreted with caution, it was of interest to note that HDL-cholesterol increased by 33% in patients reasonably compliant with fat intake, which was in line with data from Westman et al [9] . However, blood pressure levels were not reduced in patients on the LCD at 24 months. It cannot be excluded that salt intake increased in parallel with ingestion of fat, as has been demonstrated in the general population [23] , leading to less favourable blood pressure levels. Unfortunately, we did not collect urine for determination of the amount of sodium. In conclusion, our findings support the use of an LCD with 20 E% from carbohydrates as an alternative to a traditional low-fat diet, if the aim primarily is to improve glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes. We achieved a weight loss of about 4 kg in both groups after 6 months based on group information on three occasions and there was only one more group meeting, which took place at 12 months' study duration. However, as in many earlier studies, compliance with the LCD was reduced after 6 months, as judged by the increase in body weight and according to food records, and it cannot be ruled out that different results could have been obtained if more effort had been made to achieve compliance with the diet composition and reduction of energy intake.all time points d For change over all time points
